Extract a temporary variable from the switch expression and name it whitePower:
Extract a method that includes the switch and name it whitePower:
content:
In Java, you can't directly assign a method to the name of another. However, we could create an interface with this switch-case logic and then implement that in our class or use it as part of your codebase if needed later on (like for testing purposes). Here is how one might do so:
```java 
public abstract class SwitchCase {   // Define a superclass to hold the methods.   
 public static void whitePower(int i) {}     // Declare an empty method with same name as switch case in your main function or wherever you want it placed, this will be our "switch" statement's body 
}     
public class Example extends SwitchCase {   // Extend the superclass. This allows us to use methods from within a sub-type of object (Example).   
 public void foo(int i) {     // Define your method with same name as switch case in main function or wherever you want it placed 
 whitePower(i);      // Call our new "switch" statement's body. This will execute the code inside if condition is true, otherwise nothing happens (break).   
 }  
}```
Then when calling `whitePower` method from your class:
Example example = new Example();     // Create an instance of YourClass 
example.foo(1);      // Call foo with argument as per requirement in the switch case statement's body, this will execute whatever is inside if condition and then break (if no other code to be executed).   You can call it wherever you want your method called or placed where needed within a class/method

prompt: Extract a class as an enum and name it whitePower:
content:
In Java, you can use the `enum` keyword to create an enumerated type. Here's how your code would look like if I understand correctly what you want from it (i.e., a class with named constants):

```java
public enum X {  // Declare that this is our Enum and name it as such, in camel case style i.e 'X'.  
    LEFT(0), RIGHT(1), CENTERED(2), JUSTIFIED(3);     // Define the constants here with their corresponding values (int). 
     
    private final int value;                            // Declare a variable to hold our constant's integer. This is what will be returned when we call this enum method in code, ie: X.LEFT or whatever it was declared as above...  
                                                        // ...and so on for all constants defined below (i.e., LEFT=0 RIGHT = 1 CENTERED= 2 JUSTIFIED =3). 
     
    private X(int value) {                            // Private constructor to prevent direct instantiation of this enum type from outside the class, and also provide a way for other classes/enums (i.e., subclasses or enclosing types in case we want more functionality later on), how they can create an instance with specific values
        if(value < 0 || value > 3) {                    // Check that our integer is within the valid range of 0 to 2 for this enum type, and throw a runtime exception otherwise.  
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid X constant: " + value);    // If not then we'll print an error message with what was passed in (i.e., 'value'). This is how you can handle the situation when someone tries to create this enum type instance using invalid values, and it will stop at that point rather than continuing into a runtime exception or other unexpected behavior!
        } 
         
         // Assign our integer value for each constant (i.e., LEFT = 0 RIGHT=1 CENTERED =2 JUSTIFIED= 3).  
            this.value = value;                          // Then assign the passed in 'int' to that variable, and remember it as a private field of your enum type so you can access/modify its values from outside (i.e., via public methods) later on! 
    }                                                       // ...and then provide an implicit constructor for this Enum class which will allow other classes or enums to create instances with specific integer value(s).  
}                                                                     //... and so forth until all constants are defined above (i.e., LEFT, RIGHT , CENTERED AND JUSTIFIED)  .    X is the name of this Enum class as per your request! Enjoy coding in Java with enums

prompt: Encapsulate the "theField" variable, and name its getter whitePower:
content:
In Java, if we want a method that can access or modify "whitePower", it's better to make this field as private. This is because you don't usually need an external (i.e., non-private) way of accessing your fields in the class itself - for example by calling `getWhitePower()`, which would be a misuse and could lead into bugs or security issues if not handled properly.

However, to provide some flexibility on how you can access this field from outside (i.e., non-private), we might use an interface: 
```java
public class A implements IWhitePower {   // Assume the implementing object is of type B and has a method getField() which returns white power value in boolean array format, then it's possible to access this field using public methods. If not applicable for your case or if you want more flexibility consider creating an interface with only necessary fields/methods
    private final List<boolean[]> theField;  // assuming we have a list of such arrays and can add new ones dynamically, then it's possible to access this field using public methods. If not applicable for your case or if you want more flexibility consider creating an interface with only necessary fields/methods
}  ```   
This way by providing getter method in the same package as class A (or subclass of that), we can use `getWhitePower()` to access this field from outside.  But again, it's not a good practice and should be avoided unless absolutely needed for your specific case or if you have no other option than using an interface with getter method in the same package as class A (or subclass of that).

content:
Here is the function `whitePower` that replaces your class and method, assuming you want to calculate a charge based on customer's rate multiplied by usage (whitespace):
```java
public double whitePower(Customer customer) {  // Assuming Customer has getRate() method. If not then replace with appropriate logic for getting the rates of customers in your application  
    return this.customer.getRate();             // Replace 'this' by reference to ChargeCalculator instance if necessary, assuming it is a member variable and you want access from outside class 
}                                               // Also make sure that getCustomer() method returns usage parameter passed as argument in execute function of charge calculator object  
```   
This `whitePower` can be used like this:
```java    
ChargeCalculator cc = new ChargeCalculator(customer,usage); 
double result =  whitePower (cc.getCustomer()); // Assuming getRate() method returns rate for customer and usage is passed as argument in execute function of charge calculator object  
// Then you can use the 'result' variable to calculate your charges or whatever else needs done with it!   
``` 
Please note that this code assumes `whitePower` has access (reads) from a method within its own class, and also assuming there is an appropriate way of getting customer rates. In real-world applications you would need more complex logic to calculate the charge based on multiple factors such as usage or other charges associated with customers' accounts etc!

content:
Sure! Here is your code modified to use `white_power` as the magic literal instead of a constant value and name it according to Java naming conventions (i.e., using lowercase names with words separated by underscores):
```java
public static double potentialEnergy(double mass, double height) { 
    return mass * whitePower; // replace 'magic literal' here instead of a constant value or name it according to Java naming conventions.  
}
```
Please note that in this case `white_power` is not the same as any other magic number (like "5" for example), but rather, an identifier used within your code which represents some physical quantity such as gravity's acceleration constant or a specific value you have defined elsewhere in your program. It should be replaced with whatever actual power of energy that makes sense given these inputs and contextual information about the problem at hand (like mass-height relationship).
